item.title: Moving away from Tailwind, and learning to structure my CSS
item.description: Moving away from Tailwind, and learning to structure my CSS

item.title: The CTF scene is dead
item.description: Why frontier AI has broken the open CTF format, hollowed out the scoreboard, and made competitive CTF performance a weaker signal than it used to be.

item.title: Malloc is NOT Magic: Let's Build it to Learn What's Inside!
item.description: Every program you've ever written or run uses malloc behind the scenes; find out how it works and why it only akin to magic!
item.author: (Dave's Garage)

item.title: Six SQL patterns I use to catch transaction fraud
item.description: The actual queries I run when I'm hunting fraud in transaction data. Velocity, impossible distances, suspicious amounts, merchant clusters, off-hours buys, and window functions.

item.title: A History of IDEs at Google
item.description: Laurent Le Brun's blog

item.title: DeepSeek-V4-Flash means LLM steering is interesting again
item.description: Ever since Golden Gate Claude I’ve been fascinated with “steering”: the idea that you can guide LLM outputs by directly manipulating the activations of the model mid-flight.

item.title: Announcing the Zulip Foundation
item.description: Today marks a major transition for the Zulip open-source project and for Kandra Labs, the company behind it: I’m stepping back from full-time Zulip leadership to join Anthropic, alongside three senior team members, and we’re donating the company to a newly created, independent, nonprofit Zulip Foundation. …

item.title: ‘No Way To Prevent This,’ Says Only Package Manager Where This Regularly Happens | Kevin Patel
item.description: Kevin Patel - Application Security Engineer @ NISC
item.author: (Kevin Patel)

item.title: Speedrun
Bill to block publishers from killing online games advances in California
item.description: Publishers would have to offer "independent" play patch or refunds after server shutdowns.

item.title: Most Meaningful Dates on the Web and for an LLM
item.description: I recently read this blog post by David Hagen who figured out why the 11th of every month in ordinal form (‘February 11th’) occurs so much less often than every other date (except September of course) in xkcd #1140 (reproduced below):

item.title: What Value Do You Provide? | Ethan Carter Edwards
item.description: In a world of increasingly capable AI, what value do you provide?

item.title: Everyone's a thought leader. Almost no one is thinking.
item.description: Thought leadership got cheap. Thinking didn't.

item.title: Playing ATARI music on Amiga for free!
item.description: How to emulate an Atari YM2149 audio chip on Amiga at litteraly 0 CPU cost, by Arnaud Carré

item.title: How I’m building a better way to understand and store legacy logs
item.description: Logslim is a log ingestion tool that runs the Drain log-parsing algorithm, separates repeating log templates from their variable parameters, and stores everything in compressed Parquet via DuckDB.
item.author: (Mihir Deshpande)

item.title: Agents have (Information) Needs — Joe Barrow
Baba is Agent – Samplesurium
item.description: Can LLMs play Baba Is You?

item.title: Hey you, start communicating! - Kev Quirk
item.description: David talks about why it's good to reach out to authors when you read their content. Even if it's just to say hi.

item.title: Systems are Everything, Software is Systems – Wilsons Blog
item.description: I was talking to a friend of mine recently and asked them if they wanted to learn a bit more about how to code, they said they didn’t want to. I followed up by claiming that what you learn from designing software is very useful generally, but couldn’t really articulate why. I’ve since thought about it a bit and I have something more coherent which I thought I would write up.

item.title: A letter from 2040 · xydac
item.description: A speculative letter from 2040. Trees running computation, mortality as a choice, novelty as currency, and the quiet moment when humanity realized the universe had been waiting.
item.author: (Deepak Seth)

item.title: Scaling As-of Joins
item.description: How we built, broke, and re-built our ASOF joins — 6x faster, half the memory of pandas, and scaled to a distributed cluster.
item.author: (Euan Lim)

item.title: Hauleth's blog - How do I write Elixir tests?
item.description:
Personal guides for writing tests that are readable and maintainable. Stuff to use, stuff to avoid, and how to organize stuff.
Furthermore, I think that mocking must be destroyed.

item.title: The language debate is back!
item.description: Notes on the programming language debate and why we may increasingly favor statically typed and compiled languages.

item.title: Keys and Values Are All You Need | nevzheng
item.description: Bootstrapping a SQL catalog on a flat key-value store, end to end.

item.title: Product Product Product
item.description: Working this past week with Jason Adams has given me a brand-new appreciation for the category of ideas that we engineers call 'product.'

item.title: Managed agents are the new Lambda
item.description: Managed agents (cloud-hosted agent harnesses) are powerful, but locking yourself into a frontier lab's platform now is risky - here's why and what to do instead.
item.author: (Martin Alderson)

item.title: Why Anthropic’s ultra-dirty deal shouldn’t surprise you at all
item.description: Perfect little Anthropic did the dirty with Elon Musk’s ultra-filthy pollution bomb fossil fuelled data centre. I am here to tell you why this is not surprising at all.

item.title: U.S. DOJ demands Apple and Google unmask over 100,000 users of popular car-tinkering app in emissions crackdown
item.description: The U.S. Department of Justice is seeking personal data on potentially hundreds of thousands of drivers who downloaded EZ Lynk’s Auto Agent…

item.title: A 0-click exploit chain for the Pixel 10: When a Door Closes, a Window Opens
item.description: We recently published an exploit chain for the Google Pixel 9 that demonstrated it was possible t...

item.title: Amazon workers are under pressure to up their AI usage—so they're making up extraneous tasks
item.description: In a new report, employees say Amazon tracks their consumption of 'AI tokens'—and they've been creating unproductive AI agents just to eat them up.
